items.q: What does Kant's categorical imperative require?
items.correct: Acting only on maxims you could will to be universal laws, and treating people as ends in themselves, never merely as means.
items.misconception: Treat others how you would like to be treated.
items.random_wrong: Do whatever your conscience tells you to do.
items.why: The Golden Rule depends on your personal preferences (Kant explicitly rejected it). The categorical imperative tests universalisability — whether the maxim can coherently be a law for everyone.

items.q: What does utilitarianism say is right action?
items.correct: The action that produces the greatest happiness for the greatest number — judged by consequences, not intentions or rules.
items.misconception: Whatever makes you personally happy is the right thing to do.
items.random_wrong: Utilitarianism says you should always tell the truth.
items.why: Students reduce utilitarianism to egoism. It is impartial — your own happiness counts no more than anyone else's. Personal happiness alone misses the aggregative core.

items.q: What is the focus of virtue ethics?
items.correct: On character and habits — becoming the kind of person who acts well, aiming at eudaimonia (flourishing) through practising virtues that lie between extremes.
items.misconception: Virtue ethics tells you which specific actions are right and wrong.
items.random_wrong: Virtue ethics is about following the Ten Commandments.
items.why: Virtue ethics asks 'what kind of person should I be?' rather than 'what should I do?'. Students try to extract action-rules from it, missing its character-based agent-focused nature.

items.q: Does Kant think an action with good consequences is morally better for it?
items.correct: No — moral worth comes entirely from acting from duty on a universalisable maxim; consequences, however welcome, add nothing to (and subtract nothing from) moral worth.
items.misconception: Kant believes good consequences increase the moral worth of an action.
items.random_wrong: Kant believes consequences are the only thing that matters morally.
items.why: The good will 'would shine like a jewel for its own sake' even if it accomplished nothing (Groundwork). Importing outcomes into Kant is the classic category error in ethics essays.

items.q: What is the difference between act and rule utilitarianism?
items.correct: Act utilitarianism evaluates each act directly by its consequences; rule utilitarianism evaluates acts by general rules whose adoption maximises happiness overall.
items.misconception: Act utilitarianism is Bentham's hedonism while rule utilitarianism abandons happiness altogether.
items.random_wrong: Rule utilitarianism was developed by Aquinas.
items.why: Both remain utilitarian — the question is whether utility is applied to acts or to rules. Saying rule utilitarianism 'gives up on happiness' confuses it with deontology.

items.q: In which 1785 work does Kant present the categorical imperative?
items.correct: Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals
items.why: It's Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals. “Critique of Pure Reason” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Jeremy Bentham. “John Stuart Mill” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many criteria are in Bentham's hedonic calculus?
items.why: Exact answer: 7. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
